name: Western Wall
description: The Western Wall, also known as the Kotel, is the last remaining retaining wall of the Second Temple in Jerusalem, destroyed by the Romans in 70 CE. It stands as Judaism's holiest accessible site, a place of pilgrimage, prayer, and reflection for Jews worldwide. Its significance stems from its proximity to the Temple Mount, the holiest site in Judaism, where the First and Second Temples stood. The Wall is believed to be the closest point Jews can get to the original site of the Holy of Holies. Throughout history, Jews have gathered at the Wall to mourn the Temple's destruction and pray for its rebuilding, seeing it as a symbol of enduring faith and hope for redemption. It represents the enduring covenant between God and the Jewish people, a physical link to their ancestral heritage and spiritual center. The Wall serves as a powerful reminder of Jewish history, resilience, and unwavering devotion to God.
subtitle: Last remnant of the Holy Temple.

keyMyths.title: Divine Presence
keyMyths.description: Jewish tradition holds that the Shekhinah (Divine Presence) never departs from the Western Wall, making it a potent site for prayer and connection with God. Prayers offered at the Wall are believed to ascend directly to heaven.

keyMyths.title: Wall's Endurance
keyMyths.description: Legend says that the Western Wall will never be destroyed, symbolizing the eternal covenant between God and the Jewish people. Despite numerous attempts to dismantle or damage it, the Wall has endured throughout history.

sources.author: Josephus
sources.description: Historical account of the destruction of the Second Temple.
sources.text: Historical account of the destruction of the Second Temple.

sources.author: Various Rabbis
